Terrific work, as always! But I'm not sure I like the UI, in particular the multiple columns. I don't want to see a proliferation of download links.

My preference is to only have a single .zip download for a region like Europe. It would combine all data we have for that region with any and all license requirements we accept. Then inside that .zip file is documentation for each single source we have with details of the license. Ie: Germany might be annotated "public domain" while Netherlands is annotated "credit King Willem-Alexander" or whatever.

In practice I think that means a separate `LICENSE.txt` file for every source directory, with details for that specific source. In some machine parseable format, or at least stereotyped enough some regexp can quickly sort out the attribution requirements.

(The only place where I can see we have to have a separate license-specific download is for any data that has ShareAlike or other viral provisions. If including a virally licensed source in a collection means that the collection itself has to be under the viral license, then we have to quarantine that source to a separate download. Apologies for the negative disease metaphor.)

It'd be nice to ask some customers what they think about downloads and license documentation.

# AWSume for Bash
- Added the prefix `AWSUME_` to all variables, since the user will have to run this script sourced, we don't want any variable interference with any other possible running scripts.
- Brought most of the functions out to the main body of the script, to make it both easier to read and more efficient. This also allows easy escape out of the script with the `return` command, since you won't be returning from a function, you will return from the script, and not exit the terminal itself.
- Used a better method of getting the credentials from the `aws sts get-session-token` command by using the `--query` option and treating `AWSUME_SessionText` as an array, so that the script looks cleaner.
- The script will now remove a cache file if it is no longer valid or expired.
- Consolidated a few `aws configure get` commands, as they are slow and take up a lot of time.

I thought about this a little when we were first coding the `sync` rules, and the reason I chose what we have now is that I'm a little worried about peru implicitly "taking ownership" of your changes. For example, consider the following flow:

1. I do a `peru reup`, and it pulls in some new line X in one of my dependencies, which breaks something.
2. I manually delete X to see what happens without it. My peru files are now dirty.
3. I checkout an older commit to do more testing. I run `peru sync`, maybe just as part of my test scripts.
4. I checkout the latest commit again, and `peru sync`.

There are two different things that could happen in this flow. With the current behavior, step 3 is an error, because I've edited dependency files relative to the _last version that was synced_. But if we change the current behavior in the way you're describing, perhaps step 3 will work, if line X was the only difference between the old dependencies and the new ones. That means that in step 4, `peru sync` will _undo_ my deletion of X. Essentially, peru "forgot" that that change was "mine" in step 3.

This is a kind of trivial example, but I worry it could lead to surprising behavior under the right conditions. Since the `--force` flag is a relatively clean way to make this work today, I lean towards keeping the current defaults.

I'm also curious whether you've tried a workflow based on `peru override`. An "edit dependencies in place" workflow is by far the most commonly requested feature, but it's also one of the very first things we decided _not_ to do with peru, because it gets very complicated very quickly. The override mechanism fits more cleanly into peru's model, and I'm curious whether it might work for you.

@jenshnielsen Thanks for looking over the PR. It sounds like one of the main concerns is that some of the attributes/methods may still be used. In the main QCoDeS repository, these attributes are not used (except for `max_delay`, see below). That being said, they might still be used in some of the forks. Depending on the attribute, this can be more or less likely. I think asking in Slack would be a good idea. We could then opt to keep only the attributes/methods that are likely still used as deprecated attributes, or we could keep all of them. The latter might cause quite a bit of code overhead though, especially since some of the old functionality (such as `validate_and_sweep`, `no_getter/setter`) do not fit well with how the get/set methods have been rearranged.

I can add logging to the parameters, and once all the deprecations etc have been solved I will go over the tests and docstrings


A list of parameters/attributes/methods that have been deprecated/removed:

**Removed parameters**
- `StandardParameter`: Remains as deprecated parameter, though all its functionality is encompassed in the Parameter. 
- `ManualParameter`: Remains as deprecated parameter, though all its functionality is encompassed in the Parameter. 

**Removed attributes**
- `max_delay`: Still used in QuTech IVVI, though I haven't figured out what `max_delay` actually does. We should ask why they use it.
- `delay_tolerance`: Haven't found a case where it actually does something.
- `delay`: Belonged to StandardParameter, still kept as deprecated attribute.
- `has_get` and `has_set`: With the wrapper this is replaced by `hasattr`. Might still be used in other forks.
- `full_name`: Replaced by ``str(parameter)`. Not sure if this is used, currently kept as deprecated

** Removed methods**
- `no_getter/setter`: These used to be used in the `_get/set` in StandardParameter. They are now unnecessary since the get/set is only defined in the StandardParameter if an explicit `get/set_cmd` is provided. I doubt these are ever used.
- `validate_and_set/sweep`: Functionality is now replaced by get/set_wrapper. Does not seem likely that they are used, since they were low_level functions called by get or set. Nevertheless might be worth asking on Slack
- `get/set_step`: Worth asking if it's used
- `get/set_delay`: Worth asking if it's used

But for now, we can probably be satisfied with the one we already have:

The workaround I found is to load the vectors into SpaCy first, then use a function to convert it to numpy arrays:
```
def get_embeddings(vocab):  # load spacy.model.vocab object
    nr_vector = max(lex.rank for lex in vocab) + 1
    vectors = np.zeros((nr_vector + 1, vocab.vectors_length), dtype='float32')
    for lex in vocab:
        if lex.has_vector:
            vectors[lex.rank + 1] = lex.vector / lex.vector_norm
    return vectors
```

@luca-mastrostefano I am not entirely sure what you mean by "scalar of a vector", are you referring to the norm of the word vectors?

The reason why the cosine distance works so well in practice is still a bit mysterious -- like a lot about word vectors. The cosine distance works usually better than other distance measures because the norm of the vector is somewhat related to the overall frequency of which words occur in the training corpus.  The direction of the vector and inherently the cosine distance is unaffected by this, so a common word like "frog" will still be similar to a less frequent word like "Anura" which is it's scientific name.

The cosine distance is equivalent to the inner product of the [normalized word vectors](https://www.khanacademy.org/computing/computer-programming/programming-natural-simulations/programming-vectors/a/vector-magnitude-normalization) (e.g scaling the vectors so their length becomes equal to 1.) You can **very loosely** interpret the inner product of word vectors to be an approximation of the [pointwise mutual information (PMI)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pointwise_mutual_information) between the two words they represent. This is also an avenue researchers took in order to relate the neural word vectors to the older word embeddings resulting from a low-rank approximation of a co-occurrence matrix via [SVD](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Singular_value_decomposition). That the inner product relates to the PMI between the vectors is for the most part an empirical result and there is very little theoretical background behind this finding.

If you want to learn more about it, I recommend having a look at [this paper](https://transacl.org/ojs/index.php/tacl/article/download/742/204) by Sanjeev Arora et al. and also [this one](https://papers.nips.cc/paper/5477-neural-word-embedding-as-implicit-matrix-factorization.pdf) by Omer Levy and Yoav Goldberg who have looked at your question and related topics in detail.

@oruebel According to h5py.File documentation [here](http://docs.h5py.org/en/latest/high/file.html), if the file exists, in the write mode it will try to truncate. On Windows though if that file is open, operating system won't let h5py to delete and recreate that file. It was raising an error on my builds. It was not executing the tests, thus tearDown method was not getting called at all. I had to solve that problem on the setUp phase. Because once we created the file and it was open by the Python process, the OS was blocking anything else to delete that file. My workaround was using NamedTempFile as a temporary path generator, get the flie path it creates, dump the file and let h5py recreate a fresh file with the path generated by NamedTempFile. @oruebel Does this look good now?

`proposal to separate write_plumb_data into write_iterator_data and get_data_iterators`

Yes, that's a good idea. I wasn't sure if there would be an issue with someone creating a new data group between `write_iterator_data` and `get_data_iterators` (if that's even allowed). But, if it's allowed we can just make it clear in the docs that creating a new group requires calling `get_data_iterators` again, otherwise it won't be written. Either way we can have `def write_iterator_data(iterators=None):...`, so that if the user doesn't provide the iterators and it's None, we do `iterators = get_data_iterators()`.

`Won't the user have to call plumb_data in a loop?`

I'm not sure if I missed something here, or if I wasn't clear about the queue and plumbing. The idea, and what actually happens in `write_plumb_data`, is that when a user calls it, all the data that has been enqueued up to that moment is written then. So the user should know, if there's data that has been enqueued, but they haven't called `write_iterator_data`, then the data isn't written. But once they call `write_iterator_data`, **all** the data is written. When implementing `DataChunkStream`, I tried to make absolutely sure that no data will be unwritten once `write_plumb_data` is called. So the user will not need to call it in a loop, only once whenever they want to write the currently unwritten data. Does this resolve the issue? I also didn't understand what you meant about updating the `HDF5IO` API.

`How do you know right now when you need to stop calling plumb_data`

The same as the last, you only need to call it once, and it writes all the currently unwritten data. So if you appended data to the `DataChunkStream` you know you need to call `write_iterator_data` and it'll write everything. Specifically, every time `write_iterator_data` is called, `__iter__` of the `DataChunkStream` is called and it iterates over **all** the data appended up to now.

`When do you think I/O should be flushed?`

I'm not sure I understood this. I assumed that as we edit the hdf5 data, hfpy will automatically flush the data to disk? Is that not the case? Will it just remain in RAM if we don't flush it explicitly? If so, I agree we should have a flush parameter.

`About hdf5 data corruption and fragmentation issues`.

I'm not too familiar with these issues as I haven't stress used hdf5 yet, although I'd admit I always worried how I can make sure that new data doesn't corrupt the file. In past circumstances I handled it by creating a backup every few minutes. But I'm not sure why you're worried here about corruption anymore than a normal `write`, which also writes many channels and groups? Are you worried about causing an exception during streaming? About fragmentation, aren't we passing it on from HDF5 to the OS/disk? Does HDF5 not deal well with fragmentation compared to OS? I guess that may explain the slow loading time in some of my hdf5 data with e.g. 60 electrodes.

Either way your idea about optionally creating a new file for each stream sounds great, although I'm not sure how to do this myself.

I can definitely add examples and a tutorial.

There are a couple of challenges with using tags for special functionality
in the core software:

* Tags alone are not sufficient as they can only encode very simple
information. If we embrace the usage of tags for official functionality, we
will have to answer the "tag or metadata?" question for each and every bit
of official data. From an organizational and communication perspective, it
is much easier to make the decision a single time.
* Any special functionality of core jupyter software needs to be in the
notebook format and official documentation. It is simpler conceptually to
not have a portion of our spec that get's into particular tags. Also, we
don't want to communicate to others that they can ask us to support
additional official tags.
* It is easier to deal with metadata than tags from a code perspective.

Many (most?) extensions do not depend on the kernel, so it wouldn't make sense to install them per-kernel. Part of the point of nbextensions is to provide functionality that works across different kernels.

Kernels get a `kernel.js` file which is loaded in the frontend when the kernel starts. Customisation can be done there. See e.g. [IRkernel's kernel.js](https://github.com/IRkernel/IRkernel/blob/master/inst/kernelspec/kernel.js). You can also put files in the nbextensions directory but not configure them as an nbextension, and then use a `require()` call to load them - either from `kernel.js` or from Javascript output produced by running code. [Mobilechelonian](https://github.com/takluyver/mobilechelonian) uses this technique, for example.

## Creating Sigasi Projects
The official Sigasi [SigasiProjectCreator](https://github.com/sigasi/SigasiProjectCreator) utility can be used to generate a Sigasi project from a VUnit project using the following script. The script will create virtual folders for each VHDL library containing the files of the library.
```python
from SigasiProjectCreator import SigasiProjectCreator
from os.path import relpath, exists, basename
import os
from shutil import rmtree

def create_sigasi_project(prj, name, project_path):
    """
    Create Sigasi project from VUnit object prj with name into project_path
    """
    creator = SigasiProjectCreator(name, 2008)
    
    for source_file in prj.get_project_compile_order():
        file_name = source_file.name
        library_name = source_file.library.name
        virtual_name = library_name + "/" + basename(file_name)
        creator.add_link(virtual_name, file_name)
        creator.add_mapping(virtual_name, library_name)

    if not exists(project_path):
      os.makedirs(project_path)

    creator.write(project_path)

```

The API, as discussed in #1187, will be as follows:
```Python
X[:, :, join(Y, on="key", how="inner")]  # i.e. X.key == Y.key
X[:, :, join(Y, on=["xkey", "ykey"])]
X[:, :, join(Y, on=(f.xkey == g.ykey))]
```
The parameter `how` takes values `inner`, `outer`, `left` and `right` (same as in pandas), with "inner" being the default.

Ah yes, this is an open problem when using storage and auth together. We need to figure out a good way to handle it.

The underlying issue is when storage is enabled, django-eventstream instructs the proxy to send recovery requests occasionally (by providing a "next link"), and such recovery requests don't contain the original request headers. The reason the proxy omits the original request headers is because the next link is intended to be potentially sharable by multiple users to enable request collapsing at the proxy. This means the next link ideally contains non-user-specific creds to access the necessary channels.

The quick fix is for your app to just set the cookie value as a `request.GET` parameter so that it gets applied to the next link. This would be best done in a middleware that runs before user auth. Basically the middleware could do something like this: if `sessionid` query param present, put it in `request.COOKIES`, else if `sessionid` cookie present add it to `request.GET`. All users will end up with unique next links so they won't be sharable, but that may be just fine and it should get the job done.

If multiple users listen to the same channels, the more optimal solution would be to set shared creds on the next link so that all users have the same next link, and then `get_channels_for_request` authorizes by user or shared creds depending on what is provided. We might be able to make this less awkward by adding more methods to ChannelManagers, but need to think about it.

```python
import botocore.session
from botocore.utils import fix_s3_host

session = botocore.session.get_session()
client = session.create_client('s3')
client.meta.events.unregister('before-sign.s3', fix_s3_host)
```

Hmmm. After further investigation, this is the ARN generated by the managed policy:

```arn:${AWS::Partition}:es:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:domain/${domainName}```

But this is the ARN used [elsewhere](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/security/how-to-control-access-to-your-amazon-elasticsearch-service-domain/):

```arn:${AWS::Partition}:es:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:domain/${domainName}/*```

After swaping out the managed SAM policy for a standard IAM policy with that ARN, it started working.
Another option is to keep using the managed `ElasticsearchHttpPostPolicy` policy but set `domainName` to `your-domain-name/*`.

1. 0 means 0 (not any). What it means is that the first multi-pattern 
gets instantiated eagerly (the first in your list of patterns). The others are not.
2. So yes, you could write the same quantifier with different multi-patterns 
and Z3 would act as if it works eagerly with an unlimited number of multi-patterns. This is so as pointed out in 1: Z3 treats the first pattern in the list of patterns more prominently as other multi-patterns that occur later.
3. Note that a multi-pattern is a :pattern directive with more than one term in the list. It is not the same as multiple :pattern directives. In your example, you have two multi-patterns, each of them contain two terms. So when you say "duplicating" axiom, it does not eliminate what I call a multi-pattern. It eliminates the use of several patterns (or say, multi*ple* patterns).  

Multi-patterns are generally expensive. The reason is that the number of potential matches grows exponentially with the number of terms in the pattern. For example if we have the multi-pattern:

      ( (edge x y) (edge y z) (edge z u) (edge u v))

Then the number of matches grows (potentially exponentially) with the number of different possible paths.

For your purpose I suggest to simply increase the number from 0 to the maximal number of multi-patterns that you use. In this way, Z3 would treat all patterns the same.

The code snippet in the bug report is already using C++, so you can use the C++ functionality from the Z3 C++ API. You will have to start out creating a "context" object. You can access Z3_context from this one (and live with the error handler it supplies), or you can write your own version of z3++.h where you initialize a context with a Z3_context pointer and assume external ownership, say. You will still have to reconcile error handling. Furthermore, you can create separate functions for separate functionality. For example, the function that extracts conjuncts from a resulting goal would be an example of a separate function that can be written on top of the API. There are many auxiliary functions that one can think of and baking them into the APIs will not be easy to maintain across supported languages.

Z3 contains several solvers. One of them is `nlsat`, a solver for non-linear real arithmetic. However, `nlsat` does not support assumptions, it is not incremental, and it is not integrated with the other theories. `nlsat` is implemented here: https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/tree/master/src/nlsat
The general purpose solver (which is incremental, supports assumptions, many theories, ...) is implemented here: https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/tree/master/src/smt

The general purpose solver uses incomplete methods for non-linear arithmetic. 
There are some posts about that at stackoverflow:
http://stackoverflow.com/search?q=%5Bz3%5D+nonlinear

The legacy build configurator mixes input and outputs of the compiler in the same directories. I can't think of a real benefit to this, while having the output in designated folders has benefits, such as very easy cleaning and separation of builds. The cmake system does not prevent auxiliary files, other than ones where the include paths would cause ambiguities. The legacy build configurator isn't compelling compared to cmake, which supports a very flexible set of back-end build systems and it will be awesome  when we can properly sunset those parts of the legacy configurator that deal with build configuration.

Adding the x86 Windows version as a package gets my assembly to compile properly regardless of platform---I guess the compiler can still get the right metadata out of `Microsoft.Z3.dll`!---but actually trying to _use_ the x86 Windows build runs into the probably all too expected problem of it trying to reference half of Windows.

```python
# in tools.py
def lambertw_maybe_log(argW, logargW):
    # scipy's result
    lambertwterm = lambertw(argW).real

    # Three iterations of Newton-Raphson method to solve
    # w+log(w)=logargW. The initial guess is w=logargW. Where direct
    # evaluation (above) results in NaN from overflow, 3 iterations
    # of Newton's method gives approximately 8 digits of precision.
    w = logargW
    for i in range(0, 3):
        w = w * (1 - np.log(w) + logargW) / (1 + w)
    lambertwterm_log = w

    lambertwterm = np.where(np.isfinite(lambertwterm), lambertwterm,
                            lambertwterm_log)

    return lambertwterm

def v_from_i(...):
    # ...
    argW = I0 * Rsh / nNsVth * np.exp(Rsh * (-I + IL + I0) / nNsVth)

    # Calculate using log(argW) in case argW is really big
    logargW = (np.log(I0) + np.log(Rsh) - np.log(nNsVth) +
               Rsh * (-I + IL + I0) / nNsVth)

    lambertwterm = tools.lambertw_maybe_log(argW, logargW)
    # ...

def i_from_v(...):
    # ...
    argW = (Rs*I0*Rsh *
            np.exp(Rsh*(Rs*(IL+I0)+V) / (nNsVth*(Rs+Rsh))) /
            (nNsVth*(Rs + Rsh)))

    logargW = np.log(...) + np.log(...) + ...

    lambertwterm = tools.lambertw_maybe_log(argW, logargW)
    # ...
```

For ARM cmdlets helps you to get metrics data. 
https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/mt282452.aspx 


For ASM, retrieve metric data from diagnostic storage- 
```
$table = Get-AzureStorageTable -Name $table_name -Context $diagnostic_storage_context

$query = New-Object Microsoft.WindowsAzure.Storage.Table.TableQuery

#Define columns to select.
$list = New-Object System.Collections.Generic.List[string]
$list.Add("RowKey")
$list.Add("IntValue")

#Set query details.
$query.SelectColumns = $list
$query.TakeCount = 20

#Execute the query.
$entities = $table.CloudTable.ExecuteQuery($query)
```

Hm. But this is only a very small part of what a azure-storage can actually offer.

Hi @Cherishty, thanks for reaching out!

I understand that you have a need for uploading/downloading entire directories, but I do not believe the SDK is the right place for that functionality. This is because the SDK is only supposed to be a thin wrapper over the [REST API](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/storageservices/file-service-rest-api), so the user should be responsible for enumerating the directory and making the decisions about the behavior(ex: whether or not to follow symbolic links). Listing directories should be [straightforward](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3207219/how-do-i-list-all-files-of-a-directory).

If you would like to use a tool to upload/download directories, please consider [AzCopy](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-use-azcopy), or the experimental [AzCopy V2](https://github.com/Azure/azure-storage-azcopy). Here is a [guide](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/storage/common/storage-use-azcopy#upload-files-to-an-azure-file-share).

Hello!

> Awesome library, I've been studying it and the care with its design and implementation is something to behold, congratulations to @njsmith and the team!

Thanks! And sorry for the slow response...

> At work we use Qt to develop desktop applications and I was wondering if it is possible to use Qt's event loop instead of the internal `trio` event loop, something like quamash does for asyncio.

Yeah, Trio+GUI is an important question! Also... kind of complicated. Right now there isn't anything implemented. Obviously this should change. I have very little experience writing GUI apps, so I'm not sure I know enough to recognize a good solution when I see it. So it's tricky.

Well, if you look around the issue tracker, you'll see that Trio follows a thinking-out-loud-driven development methodology, and we don't have a ticket yet for GUI stuff, so let's start with a brain dump of some things I've been thinking about :-).

First, if you just want to get something working, the shortest path to do that will be to run Qt in one thread and Trio in another. It's not too hard to build some infrastructure to call between them, similar to what `trio.BlockingTrioPortal` does for calling between Trio and a thread running synchronous code – I can give you some tips if you like. This isn't exactly ideal, basically because it involves threads and threads are never ideal. In particular, if you have actual logic running simultaneously in the Qt thread and the Trio thread, then you have to take all the usual care about them stepping on each other toes, which is too bad since one of the major advantages of cooperative scheduling is that it [makes it easier to reason about interleavings](https://glyph.twistedmatrix.com/2014/02/unyielding.html).

That said, people do manage to write threaded programs all the time, and this probably works great for lots of cases, especially if you do things like push all the complex state manipulation onto the Trio side and restrict the Qt side to just dealing with the GUI and dispatching events to the Trio side. I'll also CC @matham here, who's been doing something similar with Kivy instead of Qt, and might be able to offer some comments. If you just want to get something working, stop here, this is definitely the path of least resistance.

----

Okay, that's an expeditious hack solution. What's a real solution look like? I'm... not sure. Popular libraries like Qt are built entirely around callbacks, while Trio's whole design (and a lot of its benefits) come from religiously avoiding callbacks. So what would a "Trio-style" GUI API even look like? I'm intrigued by some of the things I see coming out of the JS world, like Elm and Redux, that are designed to build GUIs without callback spaghetti. But this is obviously a case where practicality beats purity – desktop GUI APIs currently *don't* work this way, and we're not going to redesign Qt anytime soon! This makes me a bit nervous about *committing* to a particular solution right now (e.g. by including some half-baked qt support directly inside the trio core), but we gotta do *something*. So let's think about a possible medium term solution: smushing Trio and Qt together into the same thread (so at least we avoid threading issues), while keeping their existing architectures/APIs.

There are some practical challenges here: currently Trio doesn't have the infrastructure to use a pluggable event loop; in fact it doesn't expose an "event loop" or "reactor" concept at all. Internally, of course, it does have an API for this to handle different OSes (see the `IOManager` classes in `trio/_core/`), so we could imagine having a Qt backend. But there are two reasons this API is internal: (1) I'm reluctant to commit to a particular stable form; that adds a big chunk of concrete right in the middle of some of our most complicated code, (2) it actually *doesn't* have a single fixed API that abstracts over different OSes, since that ends up exposing some messy least-common-denominator thing; instead each backend is allowed to expose the full power of the current OS. But this approach really works best when you know you only have a fixed set of backends you care about. You could imagine coming up with some way for a Qt backend to expose the particular primitives that Qt gives you, though we don't have that now (currently the set of exposed primitives in `trio.hazmat` are determined at import time based on the OS). That's doable. What gets really nasty though is that the abstraction layers on top have to know about all the different backends – so like implementing `trio.subprocess` is on my todo list (#4), and it's hard enough getting good subprocess support on the big 3 OSes, but now Qt's native subprocess primitives are totally different again, so I guess I'd need a *fourth* implementation of `trio.subprocess`? Ick.

[This is where I paused writing this for like 4 days, while mulling over an idea :-)]

OK, though... so the two things we've identified as wanting to do are: (1) run all the trio and qt *user* code in the same thread, to reduce race conditions, (2) keep trio's existing backend interfaces, and ideally implementations. So... is it possible to use a threading hack to move *just trio's I/O backend* into its own thread?

I haven't thought this through fully, but I think there are two basic approaches that might work:

- keep basically the same `IOManager` classes that we do now, and the same run loop, but implement the `run` loop as a chain of Qt callbacks, where each time it runs everything runnable, then kicks off the `io_manager.handle_io` call in a worker thread, which then eventually calls back into the main thread to schedule another tick of the run loop. This way, we're either in `handle_io`, or in a trio task, but never both at the same time, so it's OK that they're both manipulating the same data structures (in particular, the trio task queue). However, this would mean that *other* Qt code could be running at the same time as `handle_io`, so we couldn't allow random Qt code executing outside of a trio task to casually invoke trio functions, like `event.set()`.

  Basically in this approach, Qt↔Trio interactions would still need to go through the same kind of interface that they would if Qt and Trio were executing in different threads. But if they were both acting on shared data structures without locking, then that would be OK, because cooperative scheduling. (And Trio calling Qt methods would be fine – I'm not sure if Qt allows that from other threads or not. I think maybe signals are OK, but not random methods like, I dunno, `QLabel.selected_text`?)

- alternatively, something like that, but moving *just* the lowest-level blocking call into a thread – so like doing `run_sync_in_worker_thread(epoll.wait)`, except of course we can't use `run_sync_in_worker_thread` itself because it assumes a working run loop. The tricky thing here is that I think we'd have to basically rewrite the `IOManager` classes, because you can't necessarily manipulate an epoll/kqueue/select from one thread while it's running, so whenever a new task calls `wait_socket_writable` or whatever we'd have to poke the I/O thread to wake up and start watching this new socket. Or maybe this wouldn't be necessary if we were careful to make sure that you couldn't call such functions from Qt context, for similar reasons to above? There are *very* few synchronous methods exposed on `IOManager`s now, basically just the "register an arbitrary kqueue watch" and "register an arbitrary IOCP watch" methods.

Hi @blokhin,

Thanks for taking the time to contribute this data adapter, it is very much appreciated. I did have some comments on it:

1. Can you clarify in the docs somewhere what kind of data is available freely and what requires a commercial license for MPDS? It doesn't have to be overly long or detailed, but just enough that someone that stumbles upon the code can know enough to decide whether they should look more into MPDS if they don't have the budget for a license.
2. I'd like to avoid having a set of environment variables to control import of optional dependencies ("SKIP_MPDS_DEPS").  Typically we just use a try-except method. See for example this behavior in pymatgen: https://github.com/materialsproject/pymatgen/blob/master/pymatgen/io/babel.py . Overall, most of the Materials Projects codebases are moving (even if slowly) towards reducing use of environment variables.
3. It would be preferable, although not required, to perform the ASE functions in pymatgen instead. We have nothing against ASE, it's just that pymatgen is already a required dependency for matminer so users will already have that installed. If users need to additionally install ASE in order to use MPDS adapter, it will be another hurdle for them to cross in order to get this piece of code working. Generally, I would encourage the use of ASE only in "heavyweight" cases where the functionality is either missing or difficult/awkward to implement in pymatgen. Skimming the code, I didn't see that to be the case here. What do you think?

Thanks again!

This is a very good question. The only reason I am aware of why these techniques were needed was that **in 2006 (more than 10 years ago!), Adobe InDesign CS2** only checked for the presence of the `GSUB` table, and if it was not present in the font, it did not at all process the `GPOS` table, which resulted in no kerning being applied. [Back then, I asked](http://www.typophile.com/node/29653) @readroberts to add the functionality to `makeOTF` that, if there was only `GPOS` features in the FEA file (e.g. just the `kern` feature), that a "stub" `GSUB` table was also added, in the form of: 

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<ttFont sfntVersion="OTTO" ttLibVersion="3.6">

  <GSUB>
    <Version value="0x00010000"/>
    <ScriptList>
      <!-- ScriptCount=0 -->
    </ScriptList>
    <FeatureList>
      <!-- FeatureCount=0 -->
    </FeatureList>
    <LookupList>
      <!-- LookupCount=0 -->
    </LookupList>
  </GSUB>

</ttFont>
```

So, no scripts, lookups or features. 

I don’t think the reverse is needed. I mean, I’m not aware of any scenario where, if `GPOS` wouldn’t exist, `GSUB` wouldn’t be processed — this is a more widely tested scenario because many monospace fonts and also CJK fonts include `GSUB` but no `GPOS`. 

That is all based on my own experience, perhaps there is some other rationale to put "somewhat empty" `GSUB` and `GPOS`. But I don’t think that this technique should be "abused" unless there is a really good reason. 

On the OpenType list, some years ago, I proposed the idea that the `ScriptList` in `GSUB` and `GPOS` could be used for "informational" purposes, i.e. scripts that are covered by a font’s glyph repertoire could be included in the `ScriptList` even if no lookups were associated with them. But people didn’t like this idea, and in retrospect I also think it would have been wrong, especially since there are at least two tables (`GSUB` and `GPOS`), and it would be confusing if various implementers were to query the `ScriptList` of just one of them, or both. 

So I don’t think the `GSUB` or `GPOS` `ScriptList` should be used for "informational" purposes. And I don’t think an empty `GPOS` should be inserted for fonts that only really use `GSUB`. And the insertion of an empty `GSUB` if only `GPOS` exists was only done to support an ancient version of InDesign (CS2), and the problem was corrected in later versions of InDesign (I think CS4 and newer no longer relied on this "hack"). 

So, unless there are some reasons I’m not aware of why putting these stub tables is still needed, I would not do it at all.

`'laststatus'` is a user setting, having it have particular value is not required to use powerline, that only controls when it is seen, so I don’t want to see it directly in any code blocks which tell how to enable powerline: documentation there is closer to technical documentation then to a user guide in any case. There are also another issues:

1. The code block you just edited is not a single variant of enabling powerline.
2. `#` is not a comment character in VimL.

And note stands out more then code block if somebody actually reads documentation.

Also note that even if you remove `if` and `fi` lines entirely powerline is not going to fail drastically: just when you used a pipe you will no longer see more then one status. So do not perform any tests which are not showing more then one status yourself, better check `true | false` which should show you something like

![0 < 1](http://img-fotki.yandex.ru/get/220200/9151298.7/0_c47ea_a1aba88c_orig.png)

(with arrows in different direction, of course). If you get only `1` out of `true | false` then you have broken the code.

You should treat this as either fish bug or your configuration error. Two facts:

1. Powerline supports outputting line above the prompt.
2. If powerline does this and it occupies the *whole* line including the last column this line will not be displayed.

Fish bindings do not know whether there is above line. You can though tell them to ignore above line even if it is defined in configuration: set `POWERLINE_NO_FISH_ABOVE` or `POWERLINE_NO_SHELL_ABOVE` configuration variable to 1: this way bindings will not add space that is needed only to make above lines align with below ones.

If you happen to switch to zsh fixing will be different because space is added by zsh; also fix may actually cause visual bugs (zsh terminal handling is rather different and they have space for a reason). In tcsh it is impossible to fix (though I do not know anybody willing to switch to it, especially from modern shells like zsh/bash/fish).

Powerline does not provide any scripts to install it, it is supposed to be installed by some package manager (pip, easy_install or system package manager) which also naturally handles the removal (installation script does not do any automatic edits to any configuration files). If you installed powerline with `install.sh` then you need to go to the maintainer of `install.sh`, not here.

You need to list package contents and search for `powerline.vim` there. But ubuntu packages are not maintained here, so if it indeed does not contain `powerline.vim` and not just placed it elsewhere then you need to file a bug to the maintainer. In any case alternative method which calls `:python` directly should be working fine.

After spending more than three hours on my macOS Sierra install, I successfully configured angr with Unicorn-engine support. Here is what I did.

- Install virtualenv using pip. Create a new virtual environment `angr`.
- `brew install pkg-config libffi glib`
- `pip install angr` to install the latest version from PyPI.
- Fix `capstone` install by moving `/Users/<username>/.virtualenvs/angr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/Users/<username>/.virtualenvs/angr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/capstone/libcapstone.dylib` to `/Users/<username>/.virtualenvs/angr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/capstone/`. This is an issue of capstone PyPI package. We might submit a PR to them later.
- Clone [our fork of unicorn](https://github.com/angr/unicorn) and do `pip install .` to install the package.
- Uninstall SimuVEX by running `pip uninstall simuvex`, clone SimuVEX from https://github.com/angr/simuvex, and then run `python setup.py build` to build and `python setup.py install` to install SimuVEX with unicorn support. I'm not sure why `pip install simuvex` after `pip uninstall simuvex` still does not automatically compile with unicorn support.
- Now `import angr` should not complain anymore.

Zenodo migration I handle with:
1) Import JSON without $schema
2) Migrate JSON by adding $schema and making sure it validates.
3) Index the records.

What about that you just make a dummy schema:
records/dummy-v1.0.0.json
and a normal schema:
records/record-v1.0.0.json

The dummy schema could allow everything and hence validate your bad records. RecordIndexer will put them to their own index, but add the alias `records` so they are searchable.

Anyway, if a record doesn't validate according to your schema ``records/record-v1.0.0.json`` you risk that then your ES mapping will also get messed up.

Yes, all the python script does is externally call a c# script to change the state of an object. This specific function can be found in the BaseFPSAgentController.cs script. Look for closeSimObj() and openSimObj().

The open and close functions only toggle a binary animation state, so if you needed an object with more than two animation states, you would need to write a custom script that grabs the Animator component on a sim object and then sets the animation state integer parameter on that Animator to whatever state is required. 

See this documentation for more information about Animator.SetIngeter(): https://docs.unity3d.com/ScriptReference/Animator.SetInteger.html

Just a quick clarification here, based on trial and error (as it seems like the documentation doesn't mentioned it):

**pipenv lock** -  Will create a `.lock` file based on the specification on the `pipfile`. It won't install anything on the virtual environment. 

**pipenv sync** - Reads the `pipfile`file, create/updates the `pipfile.lock` file, and install the packages.

**pipenv update** - According to the `--help` information, it runs `pipenv lock` and then `pipenv sync`. But isn't it exactly what `pipenv sync` already do?

**pipenv install** - If package name is being specific, it will add this package to the `pipfile` and install it (sort if like manually adding the package name to `pipfile` and running `pipenv sync`). I'm not sure what `pipenv install` does without package name, because it looks like it doing the exact thing like `pipenv sync`?

Contributions are always welcomed :) Here’s my typical workflow:

* `pipenv --python=...` to bootstrap
* `pipenv install` (or `install --dev` depending on the project)
* Manage packages by either
    * `pipenv install/uninstall [--dev] <package>` to manage packages
    * Manually update Pipfile and then run `pipenv install [--dev]` without arguments
* On another machine, run `pipenv sync [--dev]` after pulling in changes to keep the local env up-to-date
* `pipenv update` to update locked dependencies from time to time to keep them modern
    * Other machines would see the lockfile update when pulling in changes, and run `pipenv sync [--dev]`
* Prod can either be treated as “just another computer” and simply use the usual workflow, or use the `--deploy` flag to install/sync globally (without a virtual environment).

Thanks for pointing out this discrepancy. I'd like to make some tweaks to wording, but I'll use your contribution as a basis.

It turns out it was my fault, not setuptools'...

I was doing `#define Py_LIMITED_API` when it was too late, i.e. after `#include <Python.h>`.
Whereas, it must be defined before `Python.h` is included. That's because the latter in turn includes the `pyconfig.h` header which on Windows has an MSVC specific compiler directive to link with the correct import library when compiling extension modules and `Py_LIMITED_API` is defined: `#pragma comment(lib,"python3.lib")`

If I move the `Py_LIMITED_API` definition at the top before including the `Python.h` header, then I can successfully compile an extension module that can be imported by multiple versions of CPython 3.x on Windows.

One thought I had, which I've been meaning to try when I get time, was to simply check at the start of virtualenv, whether the request was one that could be satisfied by the core venv library (basically that means there's no `-p` option, and none of the more esoteric options like `--relocatable`, plus we're using a version of Python that has `venv`). If so, then simply defer to venv. That may well result in cosmetically different behaviour (venv is less verbose, the resulting layout of the virtual environment is slightly different) but the end result should be a working virtualenv anyway (modulo corner cases like environments that virtualenv supports but venv doesn't).

This is basically an alternative implementation strategy for @ncoghlan's "Option 1", with a lot less complexity than @dstufft's approach, at the cost of a significantly higher risk that we break backward compatibility (albeit only in ways that can be argued as "implementation details" now that venv exists).

Treating this less as "rewrite virtualenv to use the core machinery" and more like "make virtualenv a frontend for core venv, with a fallback to the old code where needed" seems to me to be lower-risk in the long term.

I think that confirms that the problem is that we don't wrap the standard math library. If we did wrap the math library, you could replace `from math import pow` with something like `from autograd.math import pow`, analogous to the numpy case.

Because we do override binary infix operators (like `**`) and we wrap `np.power`, the way to do this with autograd now is to use those instead of `math.pow`. However, for completeness maybe we could also offer `from autograd.math import pow`. (The math library isn't too big, and I think we could just copy the vjp definitions from the corresponding numpy ones.) Would that be advantageous for your use case?

In case you find this interesting, here's how we could wrap `math.pow` (warning: untested code!):

```python
from autograd.core import primitive
import math

pow = primitive(math.pow)
pow.defvjp(lambda g, ans, vs, gvs, x, y, *args:
           g * y * pow(x, y-1, *args), argnum=0)
pow.defvjp(lambda g, ans, vs, gvs, x, y, *args:
           g * log(x if x != 0 else 1.) * pow(x, y, *args), argnum=1)
pow.defvjp_is_zero(argnums=(2,))

log = primitive(math.log)
log.defvjp(lambda g, ans, vs, gvs, x: g / x)
```

However, assuming you don't rely on any bignum support (which might not interact well with autograd's floating point math anyway) or on the three-argument version of `pow`, and assuming you are willing to import numpy, it would be shorter just to write

```python
import autograd.numpy as np
pow = np.power
```

If wrapping the math library so as to provide things like `import autograd.math as math` is useful to you, we'd like to hear about it, and then we'd probably leave this issue or another one open so we can provide support for it. However, if that feature isn't actually important to you because you can just use infix operators or numpy, then I would lean towards closing the issue.

@kbourgoin I've seen a few successful approaches to this in the past:

1.  Regular expressions (like we do with streamparse).
2.  Using `execfile` (we do this [with SKLL](https://github.com/EducationalTestingService/skll/blob/master/setup.py#L7)). 
3.  Storing it in a plaintext file and just reading that (although then you need to add something to `__init__.py` that loads from the file as well).
4.  Injecting a variable into `__builtins__` in setup.py that says "I'm running setup," and have `__init__.py` only import the subpackages when that is false.  This is what [scikit-learn does](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/blob/master/sklearn/__init__.py#L43-L49), although more for the reason that their C modules won't be built at setup time.

If you add a walk method to stix2patterns.pattern.Pattern, __parse_tree could remain private.  It seems reasonable to regard the parse tree as internal, especially if the walker method is made public.  Having a walker method might make sense, as both inspection and matching basically work the same way: walking the parse tree.  Making it public makes sense if we think others might want to write their own listeners to do their own things with patterns.  But I think the idea has been to make simple APIs and shield people from the complexities of antlr.  Do we think people might want to write their own listeners?

To me, the difference between __foo and _foo is more than convention.  To me, they mean different things.  I am used to at least three different access levels: public, private, protected (Java, for example, has one more).  __foo indicates private; _foo is the convention meaning protected; and foo is public.  If I really want a keep-out sign on a property, I put two underscores on it.  I just messed up __parse_tree (but also note that stix2patterns.pattern.Pattern was written before stix2matcher.matcher.Pattern existed, so private access made sense at the time).

The Poro King change looks good. We need to be careful about the queues with ID 4, however. The issue is that Riot created two queues with ID 4. One was "RANKED_SOLO_5x5" and the other was something else (I don't remember exactly what the name was). We had to choose one, so we opted for "RANKED_SOLO_5x5" -- see the Queue `depreciated_ranked_solo_fives`.

In the match you posted above, it says the `gameMode` is classic, and we have that in the `GameMode` enum here:

```
class GameMode(Enum):
    aram = "ARAM"
    ascension = "ASCENSION"
    classic = "CLASSIC"
    ...
```

which is different from the Queue.

The issue here is that the string "RANKED_SOLO_5x5" is used for both queue ID 4 and queue ID 420, and the 420 queue is overwriting the one with ID 4. That's why you are seeing a KeyError, and it's also why changing the name to "CLASSIC" fixed the KeyError. I had a feeling something like this would happen, but since I didn't have the data to understand what Riot did, I left the error for someone to run into, and to let us know about. So I'm glad you did! With your PR, we now have two queues with ID 4, and this could cause an issue for the `.from_id` method, so we need to resolve that before merging.

I think if we simply change the queue with ID 4 to have a different name (rather than create a new queue), this issue will be resolved. Can you confirm that working with your (very old) match history works if you delete the "CLASSIC" queue you added, and change the name of the queue with ID 4 to something else? The name of the queue shouldn't matter, as long as it is different from everything else, so can you also try something besides for "CLASSIC" to make sure it works? Once you have confirmed that the name of the Queue doesn't matter, please change it to "CLASSIC" before committing, because I agree that that is a good name for this queue.

Also, can you just paste your code so that I can test this out as well? Messing with Queues can cause a lot of problems, so we need to be careful here.

hi guys, 
in any case if anybody is having same problem with[ alexnet.py](https://github.com/tflearn/tflearn/blob/master/examples/images/alexnet.py) as i posted before.
try some changes as given below. i got 87% accuracy, validation accuracy around 93, loss=0.12 and validation loss=0.12. This may be helpful.

I then called it as a function alexnet (as you can see)for training. the arguments were width, height (of images) and learning rate(which i selected tobe 0.0001) .




import tflearn
from tflearn.layers.conv import conv_2d, max_pool_2d
from tflearn.layers.core import input_data, dropout, fully_connected
from tflearn.layers.estimator import regression
from tflearn.layers.normalization import local_response_normalization

def alexnet(width, height, lr):
    
    network = input_data(shape=[None, width, height,1], name='input')
    network = conv_2d(network, 96, 11, strides=6, activation='relu',regularizer='L2',    weight_decay=0.0005,bias_init='uniform', trainable=True, restore=True)

    network = max_pool_2d(network, 7, strides=2)
    network = local_response_normalization(network)
    network = conv_2d(network, 256, 5, activation='relu',regularizer='L2', weight_decay=0.0005,bias_init='uniform', trainable=True, restore=True)

    network = max_pool_2d(network, 3, strides=2)
    network = local_response_normalization(network)
    network = conv_2d(network, 384, 3, activation='relu')
    network = conv_2d(network, 384, 3, activation='relu')
    network = conv_2d(network, 256, 3, activation='relu')
    network = max_pool_2d(network, 3, strides=2)
    network = local_response_normalization(network)
    network = fully_connected(network, 4096, activation='relu')
    network = dropout(network, 0.5)
    network = fully_connected(network, 4096, activation='relu')
    network = dropout(network, 0.7)
    network = fully_connected(network, 2, activation='softmax')
    network = regression(network, optimizer='momentum',
                         loss='categorical_crossentropy',
                         learning_rate=lr, name='targets')

    model = tflearn.DNN(network, checkpoint_path='model_alexnet',
                        max_checkpoints=1, tensorboard_verbose=2, tensorboard_dir='log')
    
    return model

This is because of the implementation of `all` on `SafeDeleteManager`. It calls the queryset's `all` method, however Django's default implementation does not. Calling `all` on a queryset creates a clone. Clones do not copy over cached prefetch_related lookups.

This is what the Django source code looks like (`django/db/models/manager.py`), with a comment about why it doesn't call `all`:

```
def all(self):
    # We can't proxy this method through the `QuerySet` like we do for the
    # rest of the `QuerySet` methods. This is because `QuerySet.all()`
    # works by creating a "copy" of the current queryset and in making said
    # copy, all the cached `prefetch_related` lookups are lost. See the
    # implementation of `RelatedManager.get_queryset()` for a better
    # understanding of how this comes into play.
    return self.get_queryset()
```

I removed the `all` call and it no longer re-evaluates prefetch_related lookups but I'm wondering if it was calling `all` for a reason or if it is safe to remove?

Manually crafting correct SavedModels using SavedModelBuilder is hard.

I recomend using the [`tf.saved_model.simple_saver()`](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/saved_model/simple_save) as follows:

```python
...
        tf.saved_model.simple_save(
            self.session,
            export_path,
            inputs={'text': self.text},
            outputs={'encoded': self.encoded},
        )
...
```

If you want to use the lower level APIs then you will need to setup your init op to load the variables from the saved model checkpoint instead of runinng its initializers and also configure other things such as assets collection correctly.

After thinking about this refactoring, I'm not sure of the benefits. I'm not adamantly opposed (more indifferent) - I just don't see the win here of moving where information is stored.

Previously, plugin info (name, element, title) were stored in 1 place (a JSON list). To register a plugin, a developer would just add an entry (JSON object) to a list and import its dashboard.

Now, that same info is stored across individual dashboards. Because polymer lacks interfaces, it's hard to document + enforce properties each dashboard must provide. And each dashboard has to call `registerDashboard`, which is a non-trivial function. And we maintain this complex registry ...

The previous system seems more systematic and understandable (and concise enough).

## Installing flow types for new dependencies

### Install `flow-typed` globally to work with the tool

Note: you can use `npm` or `yarn` to globally install this package

```
$ yarn global add flow-typed
yarn global v1.6.0
[1/4] 🔍  Resolving packages...
[2/4] 🚚  Fetching packages...
[3/4] 🔗  Linking dependencies...
[4/4] 📃  Building fresh packages...
success Installed "flow-typed@2.4.0" with binaries:
      - flow-typed
✨  Done in 2.68s.
```

### Install the package

You _have to_ specify the version you want to include

```
$ flow-typed install monaco-editor@0.12.0
• Searching for 1 libdefs...
• rebasing flow-typed cache...
!! No flow@v0.69.0-compatible libdefs found in flow-typed for the explicitly requested libdefs. !!

Consider using `flow-typed create-stub monaco-editor@0.12.0` to generate an empty libdef that you can fill in.
```

### Create a stub if a definition doesn't exist

```
$ flow-typed create-stub monaco-editor@0.12.0
• Creating 1 stub...
  • monaco-editor@0.12.0
    └> flow-typed/npm/monaco-editor_vx.x.x.js
```

### Include the definition file in version control

```
$ git add flow-typed/npm/monaco-editor_vx.x.x.js
```

### Run flow again to check on things

```
$ npm run flow

> nteract-monorepo@0.0.0 flow /Users/kylek/code/src/github.com/nteract/nteract-ext
> flow

No errors!
```

I highly recommend creating branches from `master`, following [github flow](https://guides.github.com/introduction/flow/) when making PRs. This PR has more commits than it should because you're building off your master branch which is out of sync with the "upstream" master. Since there are conflicts, you need to rebase your commits, which is more hassle than necessary if building from `nteract/papermill`'s `master`.

To make this simple, I'd see if you can fetch from this repository and create a new branch from here, then push that up as a new 

```
# Within the papermill repo, at the root
git checkout -b testbranch
git remote add upstream https://github.com/nteract/papermill
git fetch upstream
git reset --hard upstream/master

# Now apply changes
```

That should hopefully make a nice clean branch you can make the changes to.

I make a provisional solution to use mixcloud icon.
I created a mixcloud logo as svg format (black and white). You can find it here:
Black: https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/32321147/mixcloud-logo-black.svg
White: https://dl.dropboxusercontent.com/u/32321147/mixcloud-logo-white.svg

1. Take it and upload it to your host.  
2. Create a class code before </head> in your website. For example I used this:

.mixcloud-icon{
	width:40px;
	height:22px;
}

3. And then, add this line where you need to place the icon:
<a class='mixcloud-icon' style='background: url(https://yourwebsite/mixcloud-logo-white.svg); background-size:100%; background-repeat: no-repeat;' href='#'/>

It works, at least for me!

This type of problems also occur frequently when working inside VM, or when CPU usage is high. `uvcvideo` kernel module is not keeping up and some of the URB packets are getting lost, resulting in a corrupt image. 
There is a way to increase the number of URBs in the kernel module, but this is not a patch we can recommend (on some systems it had unexpected side-effects, and this change can never be upstreamed). This change, however, can help with the issue, at expense of slightly higher latency and higher memory consumption.

@ddiakopoulos This is makefile problem. The latest updates doesn't account for all file dependencies.

The problem reported can be reproduced by:

- checkout previous working version (say the v0.9.2 tag)
- make clean
- make
- sudo make install
- checkout master
- make 
- make install

If you do a 'make clean' after checking out the HEAD of master before the 'make' and 'sudo make install', the problem does not occur.

The latest change to the Makefile doesn't account for all dependencies so not all required files are rebuilt from sources.

My solution was to open `./scripts/install_glfw3.sh` and comment out the first two lines such that it now reads:

```
#!/bin/bash -e
# Installs glfw3 from source as a shared lib

# Make sure glfw (or any previous version) is not already installed
#sudo apt-get update

#sudo apt-get install build-essential cmake git xorg-dev libglu1-mesa-dev

git clone https://github.com/glfw/glfw.git /tmp/glfw

cd /tmp/glfw

git checkout latest

cmake . -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS=ON

make

sudo make install

sudo ldconfig

rm -rf /tmp/glfw

echo "Done installing glfw3!"
```

The problem is that we need single shots instead of a video stream. We cannot stream all cameras simultaneously in high resolution because this uses too much bandwidth and causes missing frames and errors. Therefore we start, wait for frames, and stop all cameras one by one. We cannot use a pipeline for each camera as in the multicam example as stopping the pipeline takes about 5 seconds per camera and about half a minute for a 5 camera setup, which is too much for our robotic application. So we used the FrameQue protocol of the individual sensors to get the last frame, which unfortunately does not support rs2::align. If you have a suggestion, I would appreciate it greatly!
@Brekel We use OpnMP to convert the color-stream's format from YUY to RGB, so your insights looks reasonable

The stereo cameras for the D415 and D435 are both sensitive to ALL wavelengths in the visible to near IR, following the sensitivity curve of the CMOS sensors. There are no IR blocking filters. So this means specifically that they will see visible, 950nm and 940nm. The D415 does have a Bayer pattern filter to extract color while the D435 is monochrome.  However, the Bayer Pattern filters do not block IR. 

You should be able to set exposure to low enough to make it work outdoors, but as you say you can add an ND filter. Just be careful with any back-reflections from the projector. So ideally use two separate ND filters, but be sure they are of good optical quality and not angled, or the depth will be affected, as you say.

@blink1073 I'd make the following changes:

- change the default `threshold_percent` to 0.05, or you'll always be warning. ;)
- call it something more descriptive, such as `min_contrast` or `mean_data_range_ratio`.
- add an option for the percentile stretching, and, I would say, make it 0 by default. (I don't feel strongly about the default, but I do about the user's ability to change this.)
- return a bool, rather than warn. Functions that use it can decide whether to warn (e.g. `io.imsave` would do this) or alter the image display (e.g. `io.imshow` would do this).

Other than that, I like having this function because if later one of us decides to inquire more deeply into that perceptual nonlinearity, we can just update the function.

@stefanv as far as I understand it, the task at hand here is to match two sets of features (aka descriptors), based on some cost function. 

A simple example could be two 2d point sets, A and B, and the cost function could be the pair-wise euclidean distance between them. 

The simplest solution one might think of is to try and assign each point in A to it's nearest neighbour in B - a so-called "greedy" or "local" solution. This is what is used at the moment, simple consistency checking is included in that pairings from A->B are compared with pairings from B->A. 
   
Creating the pairings in this kind of scenario is exactly what the linear assignment problem tackles, and it is a way of globally minimising the overall cost (distances) between points, even though that might not always mean minimising the cost (distances) for each point individually. 

Here's a simple example of how that might be useful: 

![untitled drawing](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1950756/28790417-db883e74-7620-11e7-9ff7-b7ca5fa31598.png)

In this example, the greedy approach would assign a2<->b1 in both directions (making a mistake as there is a drift of the points), whereas the global approach would accurately assign a1<->b1 and a2<->b2 .

> ...you mentioned that it solves issues with os.path.join(), which I already ran into recently. There it could be solved with surrogateescape...

I wished it was that simple.  
You see, the real problem is the *"implicit"* mixing of bytes with unicodes strings. If you haven't elimentated all mixing, surrogates can get you even further into trouble, because they will break *later*, when some half-bytes + half-surrogate-escaped unicode-string exits the app.

Now the typical way to fix PY2/3 encoding problems is this:
- identify meretriciously all path-bytes at app-entrance points and decode them, and 
- use unicode-literals *everywhere* in the sources, so there are no implicit operations on bytes.

But if you miss something an entrance-point, mixing will still happen in PY2.   And you won't know it, unless you feed "real unicodes" from this entrance-point (ie with some TC); and that is not an exact science.

This where *future* steps in, and [forbids mixing in the first place](http://python-future.org/what_else.html).

Frankly, I cannot get involved in this now, my attention is on the "leaks" of these library.  It is good nevertheless to have discussed about it, if you or anyone else finds the time to implement such a strict separation.

My first guess is that `repo.untracked_files` should probably return the expected `unicode` instances directly.

This occurs because when `allow_other_channels` is `False`, the call to `get_allowed_channels()` in `config.py` is trying to call `get_rc_urls()` before the local `~/.condarc` has been loaded.

This can be fixed by moving the line `allowed_channels = get_allowed_channels()` to the very end of `config.py`, after the `load_condarc` call. It can also be fixed by modifying `get_rc_urls` to return an empty list if `rc` is `None`. I'm leaning towards the latter.

Try to install the latest `qt` package first before installing the `pyqt` package.

Somehow, the post-link script of `pyqt` is executed before the `qt` package is properly installed. And the post-link script tries to copy a Qt configuration file which doesn't exist at that time.
Installing them one at a time fixes this dependency issue.

Hi @nickbent, the process for transfer learning I do is as follow:

1. Create the model with the desired number of outputs (a number different from 1001 most probably)
2. Tell the optimizer to not train the variables that are not in the last layer, this is, train only the variables of the last layer (you might also want to retrain the whole graph with the initialization of an already trained model, it speeds up the training and give good results too)
3. Start the session and initilize the graph variables
4. Load from the checkpoint all the graph without the last layer (you can set the variables in the saver, even do a mapping between the names of the variables in the graph and in the file)
5. Do the normal training

I don't think compatibility is a strong argument against this. If it was a real bug that created completely incorrect results, we wouldn't/shouldn't worry about breaking compatibility by fixing it. Theano also has compatibility-breaking changes fairly often, so as long as we are recommending people use Theano master I don't see much point in us worrying about compatibility.

As I've said before (and I think @trungnt13 is implying), it would be nice if `__version__` incorporated the commit, so that people could easily check what code they are running. But if someone really cares about exact reproducibility they probably ought to fix a version of Theano + Lasagne at the start of their experiments and never upgrade. 

I'm not sure whether this particular change is necessary, but I did run into the same issue (and used the same fix) a few days ago myself. I doubt adding `epsilon=0.0` will help many people though. Are there any drawbacks to nonzero epsilon besides compatibility?

> I'm not familiar with mock, what would be the proper way to create a named layer?

I'd have tried what you did (passing `name='foo'`) to the `Mock` constructor. But reading the documentation, `Mock` actually accepts a `name` keyword argument on its own, so you can't use this to set the `name` attribute of the `Mock`. You can do `layer = Mock(...)` and `layer.name = 'foo'` instead.

This is the way the validation works now:

* Plone javascript makes an [ajax request to validate the field (Products.CMFPlone)](https://github.com/plone/Products.CMFPlone/blob/4.3.17/Products/CMFPlone/skins/plone_ecmascript/inline_validation.js#L76).
* [This view (plone.app.z3cform)](https://github.com/plone/plone.app.z3cform/blob/0.7.8/plone/app/z3cform/configure.zcml#L139-L144) [calls form extractData method (plone.app.z3cform)](https://github.com/plone/plone.app.z3cform/blob/0.7.8/plone/app/z3cform/inline_validation.py#L29).
* What calls [widget extract method (z3c.form)](https://github.com/zopefoundation/z3c.form/blob/3.2.11/src/z3c/form/form.py#L148).
* Widget [tries to convert the data into the value (z3c.form)](https://github.com/zopefoundation/z3c.form/blob/3.2.11/src/z3c/form/widget.py#L129-L132).
* In our case the converter is [set to float type (z3c.form)](https://github.com/zopefoundation/z3c.form/blob/3.2.11/src/z3c/form/converter.py#L134-L139) and [parsed by (z3c.form)](https://github.com/zopefoundation/z3c.form/blob/3.2.11/src/z3c/form/converter.py#L123) [the formatter (z3c.form)](https://github.com/zopefoundation/z3c.form/blob/3.2.11/src/z3c/form/converter.py#L109).
* [The formatter is defined in zope.i18n](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.i18n/blob/master/src/zope/i18n/format.py#L260-L327) and this is the original exception:

```python
ipdb> value
u'400,5540'
ipdb> self.type
<type 'float'>
ipdb> self.formatter.parse(value)
*** NumberParseError: Not a valid number for this pattern u'#,##0.###;-#,##0.###'.
```

The generated regex is wrong, float type can have [53 bits of precision](https://docs.python.org/2/tutorial/floatingpoint.html) instead of just 3.

Speaking of which...
> Is there anything besides total flux that you found yourself lacking while you were trying to implement the GREAT3-like sims?

- The disk_flux and bulge_flux should be included separately as well as the total flux, since these currently require non-trivial calculations.  
- The other thing that currently requires a calculation is the hlr (all 3: sersic, bulge, disk).  It's not as much of a calculation as the flux, and all the necessary information is there, but I guess it would be nice to have the hlr already there.
- And I guess while we're at it, maybe put the use_bulgefit calculation in there as well?  It's not too complicated, but there are a couple of obscure bits, so if people are using this from outside of COSMOSCatalog, it might be nice to just have a column that tells them whether they should use the Sersic or B+D model.

> The PR is asking to merge this into releases/1.3, whereas it should be master. Can this be edited in the PR or does a new one have to be made?

[Apparently not](https://github.com/isaacs/github/issues/18).  But lots of people (>1500 comments, mostly saying +1) want it.  Here is a gem from that issue thread:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/623887/15429141/6f0d824c-1e6d-11e6-8119-85017abe5915.png)

The last one here is relatively common.  When we started on GalSim, it was still relatively common for people to have computers with only 2GB of RAM, so we didn't want people accidentally thrashing their swap space from large FFTs.

However, now it would probably make sense to up the default maximum_fft_size to 8192 at least (or maybe even larger).  The current default is only 4096, which isn't large enough for some kinds of PSFs (particularly with a complicated optics component).

I'm fine with just making the API change to have preload actually load in all the data as you had expected it to do.  (Although I think the doc string describes the current behavior accurately.)

The case for preloading as we currently do it has largely gone away, I think.  Some time ago I did some timing tests with and without preloading, and there was no longer the kind of difference we were seeing back in 2012 when we were working on issue #168.  If you use all the galaxies, the times were the same, and if you didn't, preloading was slower.  So we don't promote `preload` anywhere in the demos, and I don't ever bother with it myself.

So I'd be fine with changing it to actually load in all the data, rather than just the header info, as the word "preload" sounds like it would be doing.

You can start small. I suggest that you make a backup of all this code that you have here, and restart from scratch with very little code.

For example, you start a test that inherits from `HistoryWebTest` in `test_history.py`.

In the `setUp`of this new test, you perform some operations:
* create a bucket
* create a collection
* create 1 record
* delete 1 record
(*see examples in the FilteringTest setup*)

Then you first assertion will be simple: test that you can revert the delete operation.
```py
def test_can_revert_record_deletion(self):
    # assert that the record is missing
    self.app.get(record_url, headers=self.headers, status=404)
    # call the revert endpoint
    # assert that the record is back
    self.app.get(record_url, headers=self.headers, status=200)
```

Once you get that simple bit working, you can iterate like this:
* add a new test that will fail because the code does not handle the new case
* make the test pass with some new code
* refactor and clean-up if necessary
* go to step 1

If our contributions docs do not allow to get started, please let us know and open dedicated issues, even if it's just a question! It is very important that no particular knownledge is necessary to get started!

Good luck and enjoy!

Support for RecycleBinEntry was left out intentionally.  One of the reasons for that was to avoid exposing a functionality (delete) that would destroy the last means of recovering a deleted item.  I'm reluctant to offer the rope on that aspect. But, the prospect of having a means to 'get' RecycleBinEntries can certainly be revisited.  I'd be interested in understanding the scenario in which you'd use a RecycleBinEntry 'get' mechanism.  The items in the RecycleBin are not full entities, they have a greatly reduced set of attributes, so any 'get' operation would end up returning 'RecycleBinEntry' items rather than 'Defect' or 'Story' items.

In general, I'd start small with a single-node (w/o TFoS) and a small dataset to make sure you understand the TF code enough so you can modify it for your needs.  Once you're happy with that code, you can try to "distribute" it on a small cluster (e.g. 2-3 nodes + TFoS). Changing get_db_prep_value to get_db_prep_save results in one test failing due to an int / long mismatch:

```python
    def test_foreignkey_in_embedded_object(self):
        class A(models.Model):
            pass
        class FK(models.Model):
            fk = models.ForeignKey(A)
        class E(models.Model):
            efk = EmbeddedModelField(FK)
        e = E.objects.create(efk=FK(fk=A.objects.create()))
        efk = E.objects.get().efk
        self.assertNotIn('fk', efk.__dict__)
        self.assertIsInstance(efk.__dict__['fk_id'], type(e.id))
        # ^ failure: efk.__dict__['fk_id'] is an int, while e.id is a long
        self.assertIsInstance(efk.fk, A)
```

I think it's OK to loosen the test a little bit -- the mismatch is due to ForeignKey having a no-op get_db_prep_value (inherited from Field), but defining get_db_prep_save (that calls the method on the related field, which in case of an AutoField results in passing the value through value_to_db_auto in the end). My feeling is that it would actually be good, if the value would go through the same conversion as a value of the pointed to field when it's being saved; what do you think?

There are a number of approaches to fixing this.

1. Force actor placement and GPU scheduling to all go through the global scheduler (however, this might break down with multiple global schedulers depending on how we do it).

Right, the advantage of `ray.status(object_id)` is just the the user already has the task ID, but presumably if you do

```python
@ray.remote(num_return_vals=3)
def f():
    return 1, 2, 3

id1, id2, id3 = f.remote()
```

Then `ray.status(id1)`, `ray.status(id2)`, and `ray.status(id3)` would all return the same thing.

It is possible to have something like `ray.status(task_id)`. The task ID is partially exposed at the moment through the information in `ray.global_state.object_table()`, which has a mapping from the ID of an object to the ID of the task that created it.

Because OrderedDict is generic in typeshed, it is possible to specify key and value types for it, but only if you use a stringified annotation so it isn't evaluated at runtime, because at runtime indexing into `OrderedDict` will fail. Once PEP 563 is in place in Python 3.7, even that restriction would be lifted (though not until we drop support for Python 3.6). So this isn't a simple fix, but it is possible to fix; I'd be inclined to keep it open to track the limitation? This isn't the last report of it we'll see; better if people find an already-open issue than keep filing duplicates.

@gopchandani, Hi, thank you for the question.

> Are these metrics only good for binary classifiers?

These metrics works for multiclass classifiers as well, you simply need to binarize the output vector:
```py
import keras
import keras_metrics

num_classes = 10

model = models.Sequential()
model.add(keras.layers.Dense(512, activation="relu", input_dim=128))
model.add(keras.layers.Dense(128, activation="relu"))
model.add(keras.layers.Dense(num_classes, activation="softmax"))

model.compile(optimizer="nadam",
              loss="categorical_crossentropy",
              metrics=[keras_metrics.precision(), keras_metrics.recall()])

# Train the model. Content of the x_batch variable looks an
# array of attribute vectors. Content of the y_batch looks like
# an array of labels, where label is a value in a range [0, num_classes)
#
#  x_batch = [ [0.4, 0.2, ..., 0.1],
#              [0.1, 0.3, ..., 0.2] ]
#
#  y_batch = [2, 1, ..., 9]
#
x_batch, y_batch = read_next()
y_batch = keras.utils.to_categorical(y_batch, num_classes)

model.train_on_batch(x_batch, y_batch)

# Retrieve the testing data from some source.
x_true, y_true = read_next()

# Binarize the output.
y_true = keras.utils.to_categorical(y_true, num_classes)

# Evaluate the metrics.
loss_and_metrics = model.evaluate(x_true, y_true)
```

> How does one interpret the output for precision/recall if a multi-class classifier is being trained?

I'm not sure if understood you question correctly. The precision/recall will be calculated on the evaluation step only, training does not trigger the metrics calculation. Metrics are calculated across all classes of a model and not on per-class basis.
