This is an consequence of how we implemented DyNet parameter collections (all parameters are stored in the ParameterCollection, so if it goes out of scope the parameters are destroyed, even if there are still remaining references to them). There is a simple solution: never let your top-level ParameterCollection go out of scope. But if you're not aware of this, this could lead to really difficult-to-identify errors. This is a problem in C++ as well.

I think for Python, @pcyin's proposed solution should work. It would add a little bit of overhead for each parameter, but I think it's probably worth it as a temporary fix for this bug.

And yet if I have just a little amount of secrets, I have to look at the freeish solutions anyways because why pay tens of dollars a month on tens of secrets I can easily manage with parameter store or credstash?

>    pc = pcap.pcap(name='Realtek PCIe GBE Family Controller')

That string is a *description*, not an *interface name*, and the error WinPcap/Npcap returned was probably a "no such interface" name.

As for why the message itself is bogus, `pcap_win32_err_to_str()` calls `FormatMessage()`; if it's calling `FormatMessageU()` rather than `FormatMessageA()`, the string returned will be in UTF-16, but all error strings from pcap are in an encoding in which ASCII characters are represented as 1 byte, not 2 bytes.  It should probably be changed to explicltly call `FormatMessageA()`; I'll check that into the libpcap source.

(Dear Microsoft: as there are a lot of libraries that started out on UN\*X, and thus deal with strings in encodings of the sort described above, so perhaps you need to make it easier to handle UTF-8, that being an encoding of Unicode in which ASCII characters are represented as 1 byte.  Having Win32 APIs in "ANSI", which means "ASCII plus extended characters in the current code page", and "Unicode", which means "Unicode in the UTF-16 encoding, little-endian", forms doesn't suffice any more.  There's no guarantee that every user will be using 65001 as the current code page, so that "ANSI" means "UTF-8", especially given that not everything *works* in all versions of Windows when that's the current code page.)

> Did you happen to check if the error messages are as good as they were before? Like if I have a assert obj.id == 'mid', does it still fail by giving me the left-hand side and right-hand side values, or does it just give me a generic "assertion failed" now?

Yep! Here's how it looks like:
```
E       AssertionError: assert 'mid' == 'foo'
E         - mid
E         + foo
```

For our custom `assert_requested` method, pytest still attempts to give more details than just the exception that we manually raise:
```
E           AssertionError: Expected call: request(<ANY>, 'get', '/v1/foo', <ANY>, <ANY>)
E           Actual call: request(<stripe.api_requestor.APIRequestor object at 0x11218cad0>, 'get', '/v1/charges', {})
E
E           pytest introspection follows:
E
E           Args:
E           assert (<stripe.api_.../charges', {}) == (<ANY>, 'get',... <ANY>, <ANY>)
E             At index 2 diff: '/v1/charges' != '/v1/foo'
E             Right contains more items, first extra item: <ANY>
E             Use -v to get the full diff
```

Before the error message, pytest also outputs the body of the method where the failed assertion happened. Unfortunately, for our `assert_requested` method it will output the body of `assert_requested` rather than the body of the test that calls `assert_requested`. This is likely fixable, but it's a minor QoL thing so I punted for now.

It seems I am not able to present the main idea, let me sum it up again.  Just close this PR immediately if you will not find any new information in it.

1.  There is a valid "business use case" for overriding `_requires()` in order to restrict the dependency tree.
2. When one does that, he must also override `complete()` because current implementation checks all dependencies (got by `requires()`), though one need to check only the actual dependencies (got by `_requires()` or `deps()`.

This PR deals with the item "2" -- no need to override `complete()` or other methods when one overrides `_requires()`.

### Why should I override `_requires()` at all?

Imagine a whole tree of jobs for daily batch data processing.  The root is a wrapper job representing the daily pipeline and is run every day and it imports data and performs analytics processing.

Today, we have discovered a bug in the middle of analytics processing.  We need to recompute the analytics tasks but we don't want to reimport all the source data again.  

This need is IMHO the valid case to override `_requires()`.  We declared a Task parameter `startAt` and overrid `_requires()` to exclude all tasks not affected by the given Task in the dependency tree.
Thus, we don't have to code one-shot tasks to fix data errors every time we discover a bug, effectively duplicating the part of existing task tree.  Is there a better, more proper way we should follow?

The functionality of the `atomic_output` arg is that if it is `True` (the default), the atomic `move_dir` should be used (the previous behavior). If it is `False`, then the atomic `move_dir` should be skipped and the hadoop-streaming job should instead output directly to the final directory. Having the option to disable that is necessary when allowing S3 output because there is no atomic move operation in S3 like there is in HDFS.

> How i can extract audio stream from video streaming

You can do this by using the `-O` streamlink parameter and piping the data into ffmpeg and extracting/encoding (any of the) the audio channels of the video stream. Here's a simple example:

```
streamlink -O STREAM best | ffmpeg -i pipe:0 -c:v none -c:a libmp3lame -b:a 320k -joint_stereo 0 -y -format mp3 "$(date +"recording_%Y%m%d%H%M%S.mp3")"
```

I'm not sure what you mean with http streaming and "localhost:port/name.mp3", though. Do you mean as a stream input or output? If you want to output the mp3 on a local http server, then you should be fine with the example above, which writes the mp3 file to your filesystem and a local http server can simply read from it. If the data should not be written to the file system, then it's a bit more complicated and you probably need to write a script (python, nodejs, etc.) that launches a http server, runs the (modified) command above, reads from ffmpeg's stdout and serves the data to all connections of the server.

I think the rayTest is a good parallel with what we're looking at.  The `btCollisionWorld` has been configured and then we make a query on the state of the world based on arbitrary criteria (the ray).  Imagine doing the same thing, but with points.

Right now, our code doesn't exploit the BVH that is internal to bullet.  So, when we collide `m` points against the world with `n` collision objects, we are doing `mn` collision computations.  That is fundamentally inexcusable. A `btCollisionWorld` interface would resolve a great deal: it could exploit the broadphase filter algorithm, and seamlessly exploit special case code.

Ok I hadn't caught the *mandatory* factory approach. I hence don't understand why it would be different than what we have now where we can choose between:

```py

and

```

>> the downside is that swagger cannot be an «external» option anymore. The validators are coupled to the fact that we want swagger enabled on the API. It could become problematic when dealing with several formats > > outputs (e.g. API-blueprint)..
> 
> I don't understand this part. what is an «external» option ? can you describe the problem ?

In your example, you do:
```py
@service(validators=(some_validator, swaggered(Schema)))
def my_service(request):
    # ...
```
From what I understand, the `swaggered` validator is in charge of producing a Swagger output. 
The day I want to obtain the Swagger definitions for my API, I have to change the validators of every endpoint with this `swaggered` one. If tomorrow I want API-blueprint, shall I have to add another `api_blueprinted` ? Plus, does `swaggered` validate anything from the request?

In my opinion, the Swagger/OpenAPI/Blueprint thing should be external. In other words, it should be decoupled from my service definitions.

I obtain the swagger of my API by introspecting the declared services and produce the JSON result from that. Like this — as a simplistic example:

```py

from cornice.services import get_services

def generate_swagger():
    result = {
        "swagger": "2.0",
        "paths": {}
    }

    services = get_services()

    for service in services:
        for method, view, kwargs in service.definitions:
            json_schema = kwargs.get('schema')
            # transform agnostic json schema to swagger
            # to build result
            # result["paths"][service.path] = ...

    return result


if __name__ == '__main__':
    print(json.dumps(generate_swagger()))
```

Since we open the door to any validation library, we can't rely on the fact that there will a colander schema there. And I believe that we shouldn't couple the services definition with a description format like Swagger

I can't really think of a better solution than a descriptive/agnostic service kwarg in order to achieve that...

I have a suggestion that is related, so, I am posting it here, instead of a separate issue (feel free to split it into a separate issue, if that's more convenient).

1. When I was starting using certbot, I was surprised that it didn't ask me a bunch of questions related to the certificate, which were always asked when I was generating CSRs using openssl, the questions used to generate the "Subject:" line of the CSR (C= .., ST= ..., L= .., O=..., OU=..., CN=..., .

I figured out that it is not offered because Letsencrypt is not issuing [EV](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Extended_Validation_Certificate) certificates, but only [domain-validated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Domain-validated_certificate).
I don't think it is addressed anywhere in the documentation explicitly. So, I am tagging the documentation issue #4370 here. (I know, that @pconrad-fb is trying to converge on the changes made there and commit them, so, maybe it will be a subsequent iteration step there..)

2. Moreover, since there is an option `--csr` , and it is possible to provide your own CSR (albeit with the restrictions on how that would work), the question that doesn't seem to be addressed at all is: "What would happen if I provide a CSR with the Subject: and all those fields?"
That may be worse being discussed somewhere in the documentation explicitly. 

I was still curious: will it not get validated, or those fields would be dropped (except for CN)?
`localhorst` on IRC told me that those fields will be dropped.

3. And (more academic, but probably still relevant in some real-life use cases) yet another question:
The CSR generated by certbot doesn't contain any requests for the X509v3 extensions (except for SAN), but the CA server issues:
            X509v3 Key Usage: critical
                Digital Signature, Key Encipherment
            X509v3 Extended Key Usage:
                TLS Web Server Authentication, TLS Web Client Authentication
I understand that the CA server will ignore the requests for a broader key usage (again, it is not documented that it will just drop those requests, but will issue the cert nevertheless). But what would happen if the CSR will have a request for a more restrictive subset? Naively, I'd think that the CA shouldn't object that, but I've been told (by the same helpful <localhorst>), that those requests would be still ignored.
Why is this important? If one wants to have separate certificate for, say, "server" and "client" authentication functionality, it should be possible to have different Extended Key Usage in those, and make them "Critical".

(Just in case, - I find this reference helpful for understanding these functions: https://www.ibm.com/support/knowledgecenter/en/SSKTMJ_8.0.1/com.ibm.help.domino.admin.doc/DOC/H_KEY_USAGE_EXTENSIONS_FOR_INTERNET_CERTIFICATES_1521_OVER.html )

So, it might be worth reflecting all this functionality related to CSRs in the documentation explicitly.

For some it's not feasible to try and split these files, so it'd be nice for this tool to be able to handle these cases.

Let's Encrypt provides access to two different servers, one which generates valid certificates, and another called the 'staging server' which can be used to test certificate generation, but the certificates that it provides are not valid in web browsers. People use the staging certificate for testing to avoid being rate limited by the normal server.
It seems that you created an authorization for the staging certificate which was being reused instead of generating a new one for the main server. It seems that that authorization has been overwritten now, since your renewals are working. I wouldn't worry about this anymore since this is a somewhat rare issue.

Looking at the stack trace, looks like the problem is Certbot parsing sites-available/* which includes apparently broken configuration file `000-default.conf.save`. This is fortunately fixed in the just released Certbot version 0.19, however it will most likely take a while to end up in the PPA.

Your workarounds for the issue right now would be:
1) Remove the file /etc/apache2/sites-available/000-default.conf.save
2) Run Certbot with parameter `--apache-vhost-root=/etc/apache2/sites-enabled/` to trick it out of parsing files in sites-available.

The broken issue appears to be related to debian 8 (jessie) and the packages "python-requests" and "python-urllib3".

apt-get install python-pip installs these two packages as part of PIP's dependencies.  However it will also remove these two packages as part of a apt-get purge.

Apparently, Ansible's "vmware_guest" module cannot use pyVMomi's library without these two packages installed.  Any attempt to call the vmware_guest module after removing either of these two packages will cause the error noted above.

I've fixed the broken trig output, but it remains ugly. 

The underlying issue is #698 - since you do: 
```
g = Graph(identifier=i)
[ ... add data ... ]
ds.graph(g)
```

you end up with a Graph instance with it's own namespace manager. So each subgraph generates it's own independent space of prefix mappings during trig serialisation. 

It's actually much worse than this - these graphs do not share a store. So if you tried to persist the dataset, nothing would work. 

It's our fault, passing a graph instance to `DataSet.graph` should probably raise an exception. 

Change the code to : 

```
g = ds.graph(i)
[... add data ...]
```
 and all is good.

I was carried away by trying to use the `Tab` key, but the discussion in the SO makes sense. However I can confirm that the down-arrow press jumps from Option 1 to Option 3 as noted by @jonboiser. The latest [ARIA Practices](https://www.w3.org/TR/wai-aria-practices/#radiobutton) doc indicates the following:

> 1. When a radio group receives focus:
> 
> - If a radio button is checked, focus is set on the checked button.
> - If none of the radio buttons are checked, focus is set on the first radio button in the group.
> 
> 2. Space: checks the focused radio button if it is not already checked.
> 3. Right Arrow and Down Arrow: move focus to the next radio button in the group, uncheck the previously focused button, and check the newly focused button. If focus is on the last button, focus moves to the first button.
>4. Left Arrow and Up Arrow: move focus to the previous radio button in the group, uncheck the previously focused button, and check the newly focused button. If focus is on the first button, focus moves to the last button.

So I'm going to rename the title for this issue to reflect what actually needs to be fixed, that is the focus order when navigating between the radio buttons with arrow keys. :slightly_smiling_face:

Like totally! But with one adjustment: Perhaps the sorting isn't really important. It seems that Github handles it by understanding the `v` prefix. It also seems that there is a big convention to tag this way.

It might be something we can clean up through scripting, whereby we rename all existing tags. I had some notes about this somewhere :)

But just to give an example:

```
v0.6.1
v0.6.1-alpha1
v0.6.1-beta1
v0.6.1-rc1
```

Could become:

```
stable/0.6.1
prereleases/alpha/0.6.1-alpha1
prereleases/beta/0.6.1-beta1
prereleases/release/0.6.1-rc1
```

The overall goal would be to:

1) Give a clean overview of stable releases
2) Perhaps someday be able to clear out tag garbage with a big shovel :)

But the underlying problem might also be that we just have too many releases and should consider a nightly release mechanism of some sort. So we can keep releasing stuff for testing, but won't have to do alpha-1 and then alpha-2 the day after because alpha-1 had a critical issue.

At this point, I think this issue might be okay to close - but would be interested to hear some other takes on it, since it's more of a release cycle than a tagging issue :)

Running `brew install --HEAD watchman`
```
$ brew install --HEAD watchman
==> Cloning https://github.com/facebook/watchman.git
Updating /Library/Caches/Homebrew/watchman--git
==> Checking out branch master
==> Patching
==> ./autogen.sh
      See the Autoconf documentation.
configure.ac:27: error: possibly undefined macro: AM_PROG_CC_C_O
configure.ac:28: error: possibly undefined macro: AM_PROG_AS
configure.ac:34: error: possibly undefined macro: AM_CONDITIONAL
configure.ac:75: error: possibly undefined macro: AM_PATH_PYTHON
```
Running `brew install watchman` works fine.

From `watchman-make --help`:

```
  --root ROOT           Define the root of the project. The default is to use
                        the PWD. All patterns are considered to be relative to
                        this root, and the build tool is executed with this
                        location set as its PWD.
```

All the patterns are interpreted relative to the `--root` and the default is the `$PWD`, so this behavior isn't totally wrong, but it is perhaps a little surprising in this situation.  I'd be more inclined to resolve this by adding a check and erroring out if any of the patterns escape the `--root`, because it seems difficult and complex to automatically infer the correct root in all the right situations, but even adding that check increases the complexity more than is perhaps warranted.

What do I mean by that?  Today we're just passing the patterns through to watchman without interpreting them, which is great because we don't have to have overly much semantic knowledge about the patterns inside `watchman-make`.

To support being more smart here we'd need to iterate over them, parse and understand each of them in turn and then notice if they escape the root, then retroactively bump the root up how every many levels are appropriate based on the pattern, and then compensate for that adjustment it in all preceding paths.
There's a lot of complexity in that flow, so it would be great to not have to take that on here.

Labelling this as wontfix to indicate that we don't really want to fix this; it is better to fix the source of the noisy changes rather than trying to suppress them after paying the cost for receiving them.

1) The reason that something small like mnist is slower on multiple GPUs is that the computation is so fast on such small images that the distribution system (amon g GPUs) is more expensive than the computation, and thus the distribution system becomes the bottleneck. For larger networks such as resnet, the computation remains the bottleneck and therefore the distribution system, acting in parallel to the computations, does not factor into the total time.

You can first consider the ndarray that `take` is operating on as a matrix (2D). If it's more than 2D, flatten it into 2D by keeping the first dim and collapsing all the rest dims into the second dim. For a 2D ndarray (matrix), `take` op simply extracts the rows specified by the index array and stack them one by one to form an output ndarray.

In your case, let's say `eq_flag` shape is (4,), then the output of the `take` has shape (4, 1024, 48, 64).

Here's the full working install script if someone else needs to install on Amazon Linux (should maybe also work on RHEL and CentOS)

```shell
#!/usr/bin/env bash

sudo su

yum update -y

#install OpenBLAS
yum groupinstall -y 'Development Tools'
yum install -y openblas-devel.x86_64

#install jdk8
yum install -y java-1.8.0-openjdk-devel.x86_64
unlink /etc/alternatives/java
ln -s /usr/lib/jvm/jre-1.8.0-openjdk.x86_64/bin/java /etc/alternatives/java
unlink /etc/alternatives/jre_openjdk
ln -s /usr/lib/jvm/jre-1.8.0-openjdk.x86_64/ /etc/alternatives/jre_openjdk
unlink /etc/alternatives/jre
ln -s /usr/lib/jvm/jre-1.8.0-openjdk.x86_64 /etc/alternatives/jre

#install OpenCV 2.4
#OpenCV3 includes protobuf library that conflict with the mxnet ones. https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/issues/7998
yum install -y cmake git gtk2-devel pkgconfig numpy ffmpeg
git clone https://github.com/opencv/opencv.git
cd opencv && git checkout 2.4
mkdir build && cd build
cmake -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release -DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/usr -DLIB_SUFFIX=64 -DBUILD_SHARED_LIBS=ON ..
make -j $(nproc)
make install

#install mxnet
cd ~
git clone --recursive https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet.git mxnet --branch 0.11.0
yum install -y libcurl-devel.x86_64 openssl-devel.x86_64 lapack-devel.x86_64 lapack64-static.x86_64
ln -s /usr/lib64/liblapack64.a /usr/lib64/liblapack.a
cd mxnet && cp make/config.mk .
echo "USE_CUDA=0" >>config.mk
echo "USE_CUDNN=0" >>config.mk
echo "USE_BLAS=openblas" >>config.mk
echo "ADD_CFLAGS += -I/usr/include/openblas" >>config.mk
echo "USE_OPENCV=1" >>config.mk
echo "USE_DIST_KVSTORE=1" >>config.mk
echo "USE_S3=1" >>config.mk
echo "USE_LAPACK_PATH=/usr/lib64" >>config.mk
make -j $(nproc)

#install maven
cd ~
wget http://mirrors.koehn.com/apache/maven/maven-3/3.5.0/binaries/apache-maven-3.5.0-bin.tar.gz
tar xzvf apache-maven-3.5.0-bin.tar.gz
mv apache-maven-3.5.0 /opt/
echo "export PATH=/opt/apache-maven-3.5.0/bin:$PATH" >>/etc/profile.d/mvn.sh
source /etc/profile.d/mvn.sh
mvn -v

#install mxnet scala library and run unit-tests
cd ~/mxnet/
make -j $(nproc) scalapkg
make scalatest

#install library in local maven repo
make scalainstall
#replace placeholders in the pom files (seems to be a bug that keeps ${project.version} in the pom files)
find /root/.m2/repository/ml/dmlc/mxnet/ -type f -exec sed -i 's/${project.version}/0.11.0-SNAPSHOT/g' {} +

#install mxnet libraries on the java lib path
#mvn -f /root/.m2/repository/ml/dmlc/mxnet/mxnet-full_2.11-linux-x86_64-cpu/0.11.0-SNAPSHOT/mxnet-full_2.11-linux-x86_64-cpu-0.11.0-SNAPSHOT.pom \
#dependency:copy-dependencies -DoutputDirectory=/usr/lib64/mxnet
mkdir /usr/lib64/mxnet
find /root/.m2/repository/ml/dmlc/mxnet/ -type f \
    ! -name "*pom" \
    ! -name "*xml" \
    ! -name "*javadoc*" \
    ! -name "_remote.repositories" \
    ! -name "*-sources.jar" \
    -exec cp '{}' /usr/lib64/mxnet/ \;
ln -s /usr/lib64/mxnet/libmxnet-scala-linux-x86_64-cpu-0.11.0-SNAPSHOT.so /usr/lib64/libmxnet-scala.so
ln -s /usr/lib64/mxnet/libmxnet-init-scala-linux-x86_64-0.11.0-SNAPSHOT.so /usr/lib64/libmxnet-init-scala.so

#Run MNIST
./scala-package/core/scripts/get_mnist_data.sh
java -Xmx4G -cp \
  /usr/lib64/mxnet/mxnet-core_2.11-0.11.0-SNAPSHOT.jar:/usr/lib64/mxnet/mxnet-examples_2.11-0.11.0-SNAPSHOT.jar:scala-package/examples/target/classes/lib/* \
  ml.dmlc.mxnetexamples.imclassification.TrainMnist \
  --data-dir=./data/ \
  --num-epochs=10 \
  --network=mlp \
  --cpus=0,1,2,3

#RUN GAN MNIST
java -Xmx4G -cp \
    /usr/lib64/mxnet/mxnet-core_2.11-0.11.0-SNAPSHOT.jar:/usr/lib64/mxnet/mxnet-examples_2.11-0.11.0-SNAPSHOT.jar:scala-package/examples/target/classes/lib/*  \
    ml.dmlc.mxnetexamples.gan.GanMnist \
    --mnist-data-path=./data/ \
    --output-path=./data/gan/ \
    --gpu=-1
```

Two reasons: 

- On python3 installations with example dags deployed, it crashes the system because it imports the sensor module, which imports hdfs, which imports snakebite, which is not compatible with python3, as explained in https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/AIRFLOW-1316

- The operator itself doesn't have a stable endpoint to test against, so the example dag just fails and isn't useful because of that. I've made a PR to create one as part of airflow www, but that got rejected because we shouldn't have testing endpoints in prod.

I tried initially copying the whole configuration settings but it's not possible given that sometimes configuration elements come from env variables, such as `testsection` when running tests, and this can't be serialized since they could be arbitrary. At the end, you still need some sort of logic to rule this parts out.

I did reduce the complexity of this change by always copying the configuration sections when running within the context of the subdag operator (using a BackfillJob) - the generated configuration files are always removed by the `airflow run` in [cli.py#L343](https://github.com/apache/incubator-airflow/blob/master/airflow/bin/cli.py#L343).
